coredata.dc:title: A solid-state electrochemiluminescence biosensor for Con A detection based on CeO2@Ag nanoparticles modified graphene quantum dots as signal probe
coredata.dc:creator: Zuo, Fumei
Zhang, Cong
Zhang, Han
Tan, Xingrong
Chen, Shihong
Yuan, Ruo
coredata.dc:description: A solid-state electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for the detection of concanavalin A (Con A) was constructed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as luminophore, which were loaded on polyamidoamine (PAMAM) functionalized CeO2@Ag NPs by the interaction between the NH2 of PAMAM and the COOH of GQDs. The electrodepositing gold layers were used as matrix to immobilize glucose oxidase (GOx). GOx served as recognition elements to further capture Con A by the specific interaction between carbohydrate and Con A. With the increase of the concentration of Con A, the amount of GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs incubated onto the electrode was increased and a higher ECL signal was obtained. The constructed biosensor exhibited a sensitive response towards Con A and a low detection limit of 0.16 pg/mL was obtained. Such a construction strategy provides a new approach for applying GQDs to establish a solid-state ECL biosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Graphene quantum dots
CeO2@Ag NPs
Con A
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CeO2 and CeO2@Ag NPs
Electrochemical characterization of the ECL biosensor
Selection of the best experimental conditions
ECL response for Con A detection
Stability and selectivity of the biosensor
Application of the sensor to the detection of Con A in serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM of (A) CeO2 and (B) CeO2@Ag NPs, and (C) XPS of CeO2@Ag NPs (insets: the high-resolution curves of Ce and Ag); (D) TEM of GQDs (insets: particles size distribution of GQDs). (E) The UV–vis of GQDs.

(A) CV and (B) ECL response of (a) bare GCE, (b) Dp Au/GCE, (c) GOx/Dp Au/GCE, (d) BSA/GOx/Dp Au/GCE, (e) Con A/BSA/GOx/Dp Au/GCE and (f) GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs/Con A/BSA/GOx/Dp Au/GCE in 0.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.10 M PBS containing 0.030 M K2S2O8, respectively (insets: enlarged CV and ECL curves, respectively).
The ECL responses of (A) GOx-CeO2-GQDs, (B) GOx-Ag-GQDs and (C) GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs modified electrode to 10 pg/mL Con A.

The effect of (A) concentrations of K2S2O8 and (B) pH of PBS on the ECL responses of biosensor. (C) ECL response of the biosensor toward different concentrations of Con A: 0.5 pg/mL, 1.0 pg/mL, 5 pg/mL, 10 pg/mL, 50 pg/mL, 100 pg/mL, 500 pg/mL, 1.0 ng/mL (from a to h); And (D) the relationship between ECL intensity and the logarithmic value of Con A concentration.
(A) ECL stability of the biosensor with 10 pg/mL Con A. (B) The ECL responses of the biosensor to Con A (10 pg/mL), 100 pg/mL interferences and 10 pg/mL Con A with 100 pg/mL interferences.
(A) Schematic illustrate of the preparation process of the GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs and (B) the fabrication process of the ECL biosensor.
Some analytical methods for the detection of Con A.
Determination of Con A added in human serum sample with the developed biosensor.

head.ce:title: A solid-state electrochemiluminescence biosensor for Con A detection based on CeO2@Ag nanoparticles modified graphene quantum dots as signal probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Endocrinology, 9 th People's Hospital of Chongqing, Chongqing 400700, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A solid-state electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for the detection of concanavalin A (Con A) was constructed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as luminophore, which were loaded on polyamidoamine (PAMAM) functionalized CeO2@Ag NPs by the interaction between the NH2 of PAMAM and the COOH of GQDs. The electrodepositing gold layers were used as matrix to immobilize glucose oxidase (GOx). GOx served as recognition elements to further capture Con A by the specific interaction between carbohydrate and Con A. With the increase of the concentration of Con A, the amount of GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs incubated onto the electrode was increased and a higher ECL signal was obtained. The constructed biosensor exhibited a sensitive response towards Con A and a low detection limit of 0.16 pg/mL was obtained. Such a construction strategy provides a new approach for applying GQDs to establish a solid-state ECL biosensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technique has attracted great interests in the field of clinical, pharmaceutical, and environmental [1]. And ECL luminophores are one important factor which influences the performance of the ECL sensor. Traditional luminophore materials, such as inorganic emitter Ru(bpy)3 2+, organic luminophore luminol, and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), are usually highly toxic to organism [2]. In addition, the heavy metals component in the QDs are also harm to the environment [3]. Those disadvantages of above luminophores limit their developments and applications. To improve the performance of ECL analysis and extend its application in biological analysis, it is still necessary to exploit the novel, nontoxic and highly efficient ECL emitters.

As the small graphene fragments, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attentions in various field due to the good bio-compatibility, high electrical conductivity, and high specific surface area [4,5]. Based on the performance of low toxicity, easy preparation and excellent chemical inertness [6], GQDs have been applied in bioimaging [7], optical sensing [8] and drug delivery [9]. Meanwhile, GQDs based ECL approaches have been constructed for detecting nitroaniline [10], protein [11] and DNA [6]. Among those studies, GQDs were generally added in the aqueous solution or modified with a DNA strand [2,12], because oxygen-rich functional groups contained on the surfaces and edges of GQDs make them water soluble [13]. Achieving the immobilization of GQDs to establish the solid-state ECL biosensor is a central challenge. Thus, searching for a carrier to immobilize the GQDs and improve the sensing sensitivity is necessary.

Concanavalin A (Con A), as a member of the legume lectin family, is extracted from jack bean in 1936 [14]. Due to its specific structure, Con A exhibits an ability to bind with various carbohydrates and glycoproteins, such as mannose, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and glucose oxidase (GOx) [15]. Con A has been used to analyse N-glycan expression on the cell surface for its high affinity to oligomannose [16]. Meanwhile, Con A attracts more attention as protein model because of bio-specific carbohydrate-lectin interactions, which is significant for clinical diagnostics and drug development. For example, Con A was used as a protein model to achieve the analysis of malignant tumor cells [17,18]. Thus, many approaches have been applied to detect Con A, such as, fluorescence spectroscopy [19], electrochemical analysis [20,21], and UV–vis spectra [22]. In comparison to those approaches, ECL technique attracts more attention for detecting Con A due to its high sensitivity and low signal to noise ratio.

In this work, a solid-state ECL biosensor for Con A detection was constructed by using GQDs as the luminophore. Due to the good catalytic activity, high conductivity, low toxicity and large surface area of CeO2 and Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs), the nanocomposites of Ag NPs and CeO2 nanospheres (CeO2@Ag NPs) were used as a carrier to achieve the immobilization of GQDs through the interaction between the NH2 contained on the surface of CeO2@Ag NPs and the COOH of GQDs. GOx served as a recognition element to capture Con A, and further capture GOx modified signal probes (GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs) to form a sandwiched structure (as shown in Scheme 1 ), thus achieving the detection of Con A. The combination of the immobilization strategy of GQDs and the signals amplification strategy endowed the biosensor an excellent performance, also offered a new perspective for constructing GQDs solid-state ECL biosensor.

Concanavalin A, polyamidoamine (PAMAM, generation 5, amino surface groups, 10 wt% in methanol) and poly-(diallyldimethylammoniumchloride) (PDDA, MW: 200,000–350,000 g/mol, 20%, w/w in water), GOx (from Aspergillus niger, 100,000–250,000 units/g solid) and gold chloride tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96%–99%) were provided by sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS), AgNO3, CeCl3·7H2O, sodium citrate, Na2S and 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino) propyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China) provided urea, ethylene glycol (EG, 99%) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW: 30000). Vulcan XC-72 carbon black was obtained from Cabot Corporation (USA). The testing solution for ECL measurements was phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS, 0.10 M). Con A solution with various concentrations was prepared with 0.10 M PBS (pH 7.0).

MPI-A electrochemiluminescence analyzer and CHI 610A electrochemical work station were provided by Xi'an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co., Ltd. and Shanghai CH Instruments (China), respectively. They were used to perform cyclic voltammetry (CV) and ECL measurements, respectively. During the detection process, a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 4 mm), Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire were used as working electrode, reference electrode and auxiliary electrode, respectively. 800 V was set to the voltage of the photomultiplier. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-1200EX, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) were used to analyse the morphology and size of various nanomaterials. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan).

The GQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation of XC-72 carbon black [12]. Briefly, 1.0 g carbon black was dissolved in 250 mL 6 M HNO3 and refluxed at 130 °C for 24 h. After cooled at room temperature, the complex was centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rmp to obtain an orange supernatant liquid, which was dried at 200 °C to remove the water and nitric acid. Finally, the GQDs were obtained and dispersed in deionized water to prepare the 5.0 mg/mL GQDs dispersion for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CeO2 and CeO2@Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: CeO2 nanospheres were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method [23]. In brief, 20 mL of sodium citrate solution (10 mM) was mixed with 85 mL of 0.10 M urea solution. Then, 0.45 g CeCl3·7H2O and 0.45 mL H2O2 (30%) were added under stirring. Keep stirring for about 45 min, the mixture was transferred into Teflonlined stainless-steel autoclave to react for 20 h at 180 °C. Finally, the precipitates were centrifuged (8000 rmp) for 10 min and dried at 60 °C to obtain CeO2 nanospheres.

Ag NPs were synthesized based on the literature [24] with some modification. Firstly, Na2S, PVP and AgNO3 were dispersed in EG to prepare the solution of Na2S (3.0 mM), PVP (20 mg/mL) and AgNO3 (282 mM), respectively. 30 mL of EG was added in flask and preheated for 1 h at 150 °C under stirring. Then, 0.4 mL of Na2S, 10 mL of PVP, and 3.5 mL of AgNO3 solution were added sequentially. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of sodium citrate solution (0.10 M, EG as dispersion) was added dropwise. The citrate was used as the stabilizer to prevent the aggregating, and it was oxidized to acetone carboxylate, which would chelate with the Ag NPs and make them with negative charge [25,26]. When the color of the suspension reached a brown color, the reaction was quenched with an ice-water bath. Then the suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rmp (4 °C) and washed using acetone and water for three times, respectively. Finally, Ag NPs were collected and redispersed in deionized water to obtain 4.0 mg/mL Ag NPs dispersion, which was stored at 4 °C for further use.

CeO2@Ag NPs were prepared through a layer-by-layer assembly approach. 1.0 mL of PDDA solution (1%, prepared by deionized water) was added into 2.0 mL of CeO2 suspension (0.5 mg/mL). After sonication for 30 min, the suspension was centrifuged (8000 rmp) for 10 min to obtain CeO2/PDDA composites, which were redispersed in deionized water. After that, 0.5 mL of prepared Ag NPs dispersion (4.0 mg/mL) was added and the mixture solution was kept stirring for 3 h. PDDA, as a cationic polyelectrolyte polymeric material, would make CeO2 with positive charge [27]. Positively charged CeO2/PDDA could assemble with negatively charged Ag NPs to obtain CeO2@Ag NPs by electrostatic adsorption. Through centrifugation (8000 rmp) for 10 min and washing with deionized water for three times, the CeO2@Ag NPs were collected and re-dispersed in deionized water to prepare 0.5 mg/mL CeO2@Ag NPs dispersion for further use.

100 μL of PAMAM was added into the 1.0 mL of above CeO2@Ag NPs dispersion (0.5 mg/mL) under stirring to prepare amino-functionalized CeO2@Ag NPs, which were collected by centrifuging to remove excess PAMAM. The resultant CeO2@Ag NPs were re-dispersed in 1.0 mL deionized water for following use. 200 μL of the mixture of NHS (40 mM) and EDC (20 mM) (v/v = 1:4) was added into 500 μL of GQDs aqueous solution (5 mg/mL) and stirred for 2 h to activate the carboxyl groups of GQDs. Subsequently, above PAMAM functionalized CeO2@Ag NPs (1.0 mL) were added into GQDs mixture for another 2 h reaction in darkness. GQDs would be attached on the surface of CeO2@Ag NPs since the COOH of GQDs and the NH2 of PAMAM could be coupled in the presence of EDC/NHS. The resultant CeO2@Ag-GQDs were collected by centrifuging, and then re-dispersed in 1.0 mL deionized water.

The detail of preparation of GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs composites was recorded as following. 1.0 mL of GOx (2.0 mg/mL) and 1.0 mL of above CeO2@Ag-GQDs dispersion were mixed and stirred for 4 h at 4 °C to immobilize GOx onto the nanocomposite. After that, with centrifugation the precipitate was obtained and re-dispersed in 1.0 mL deionized water for follow use. To block unspecific absorption of the nanocomposites, GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs was incubated with 50 μL of BSA (1%) for 1 h at 4 °C. Finally, the GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs was obtained by centrifugation (8000 rmp, 4 °C) and re-dispersed in 1.0 mL of PBS (pH 7.0), and then stored in 4 °C for future use. The immobilization of GOx and BSA may be ascribed to following reason. On the one hand, nanomaterials have been widely used as matrix for loading proteins since the large surface area and high-surface free energy endow nanomaterials with strong adsorption capacity [28]. More importantly, Ag NPs could provide a stable surface for GOx immobilization, because of its large surface area, good biocompatibility and electronic properties [29].

After polished by alumina slurry (0.3 and 0.05 μm), and cleared with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, the GCE was immersed in 2 mL HAuCl4·4H2O (1%) solution to electrodeposit the gold layers (Dp Au) by cyclic voltammetry between −0.2 V and 0.6 V at scan rate 0.05 V/s for 3 cycles. Followed that, the electrode was incubated with 6 μL of GOx (2.0 mg/mL) for 8 h. After washed with deionized water, the modified electrode was incubated with BSA (1%) for 1 h to block the non-specific binding sites of the electrode. The immobilization of GOx and BSA was ascribed to the large surface area and high-surface free energy of the electrodeposited gold layers, as well the strong binding interactions between the gold and the amino and cysteine groups of proteins [30,31].

The prepared biosensor was incubated with 10 μL of Con A solution with different concentrations for 1 h. Con A was attached on the modified electrode because of the specific binding between Con A and sugar residues of the GOx. After the modified electrode was incubated with 8 μL of GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs for 1 h, the ECL measurement was performed in 0.10 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.03 M K2S2O8. The measurement potential was set from −1.1 V to 0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 200 mV/s.

The morphologies of CeO2 and CeO2@Ag NPs nanocomposites were studied by SEM. Fig. 1 A presents the SEM images of CeO2, and the spherical structure with the diameter of 200–300 nm was observed, which was consistent with the literature [23]. And as shown in the SEM image of CeO2@Ag NPs (Fig. 1B), the Ag NPs, with the diameter of 30–50 nm (see Fig. S1), were successfully attached on the surface of CeO2. In addition, the elemental composition of CeO2@Ag NPs was characterized by the XPS. In Fig. 1C, the peaks at 882 eV, 529 eV, 368 eV, and 285 eV were the XPS spectra of Ce3d, O1s, Ag3d and C1s, respectively, which suggested the presence of CeO2 and Ag NPs. Above results showed that the CeO2@Ag NPs composites were prepared successfully.

Fig. 1D presents the TEM images of GQDs. The insets in Fig. 1D display the particles size distribution of GQDs and the average particles size of GQDs was 4.1 ± 0.8 nm. The UV–vis absorption of the GQDs was tested and recorded. As shown in Fig. 1E, the typical absorption peak of GQDs was observed at 303 nm, and the strong absorption peak at 220 nm was assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [32]. The results indicated that the GQDs were prepared successfully.

The morphologies of the electrodeposited gold layers (Dp Au) were characterized by SEM, and the images were displayed in Fig. S1C. As seen, with the process of electrodeposition, Dp Au were formed and distributed uniformly, indicating the successful preparation of Dp Au.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The CV curves of each modification step of the electrode were tested in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution to monitor the construction process of ECL biosensor. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the redox peaks of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- can be observed on the bare GCE (curve a). After electrodepositing Au layers on the GEC (curve b), the redox current was increased because of the superior conductivity of Dp Au. With the incubation of GOx onto the surface of the modified GCE, the redox peak current decreased obviously (curve c) due to the property of inhibiting electrons transfer of GOx. After BSA (curve d) and Con A (curve e) were sequentially incubated, the redox peak currents continuously decreased due to their insulation. Finally, the GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs (curve f) was captured onto the electrode to form a sandwich structure, and the redox peak current further decreased. The CV curves of each modification step of electrode indicated the successful construction of the biosensor.

ECL measurements were further used to confirm the successful construction of the biosensor. As show in Fig. 2B, the bare GCE displayed a weak ECL signal in K2S2O8 solution (curve a). Such a ECL response was attributed to the OOH · , which was produced by dissolved O2 and could react with SO4
·- to produce (O2)2*, then emitting ECL signal [33]. After electrodepositing Au layers on the surface of GCE (curve b), the ECL intensity increased slightly due to the fact the Dp Au with excellent conductivity could accelerate the electron transfer and also could promote the electrochemical reaction of S2O8 2−/O2 system [34]. When GOx (curve c) and BAS (curve d) were successively immobilized onto the modified electrode, the ECL intensity decreased successively. After Con A was attached onto the surface of the electrode (curve e), the ECL signal further decreased because biological macromolecules obstructed the electrons transfer [35]. With the incubation of the GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs onto the electrode (curve f), the ECL intensity increased greatly, which was attributed to the excellent ECL property of GQDs and the amplified effect of CeO2@Ag NPs on the ECL response of GQDs.

The possible ECL mechanisms of GQDs with K2S2O8 as coreactant were described as follows [36]. Firstly, ground state GQDs were electrochemically reduced to GQDs ·- , and at the same time S2O8 2− was reduced to SO4
·- radicals. Then, electron-transfer annihilation reaction occurred between GQDs ·- and SO4
·- radicals to produce an excited state GQDs*, which fell to ground state and emitted ECL signals.

In order to prove the superiority of the prepared GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs probe, we investigated the ECL response of the biosensor with different probes under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , when the GOx-CeO2-GQDs without Ag NPs were captured onto the biosensor, the ECL signal increased 628 a.u. (Fig. 3A). When the GOx-Ag-GQDs without CeO2 served as the probe and was immobilized onto the biosensor, the ECL intensity increased 2239 a.u. (Fig. 3B). However, for GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs with both Ag NPs and CeO2, the ECL intensity increased 4019 a.u. (Fig. 3C), which was obviously higher than those for GOx-CeO2-GQDs and GOx-Ag-GQDs. The reasons were described as follows. CeO2 nanospheres were functionalized with PDDA. And positively charged CeO2/PDDA served as a matrix to immobilize negatively charged Ag NPs, which would bond with PAMAM by interaction between Ag NPs and PAMAM to provide amino groups for further reaction. With EDC/NHS as coupling agents, GQDs were loaded on the surface of CeO2@Ag NPs through the interaction between COOH of GQDs and the NH2 of PAMAM. The high loading of luminophore endowed the modified electrodes a high ECL response. In addition, the good conductivity of Ag NPs could reduce the electron-relay shield between ECL emitter and electrode [37], further enhance the ECL intensity of GQDs. For the GOx-CeO2-GQDs without Ag NPs, it is difficult for PAMAM to be attached onto the surface of CeO2, thus CeO2 could not provide binding sites for GQDs. For GOx-Ag-GQDs without CeO2, the amount of GQDs immobilized would decrease. Thus GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs were selected as the signal probes in this work.

In addition, the ECL signals of GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs suspension on Dp Au/GCE in the presence of K2S2O8 has been measured. The results were showed in Fig. S2 the results indicating that the interference, from the adsorption of Dp Au/GCE for GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQDs, does not exist.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of the best experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: K2S2O8 as the coreactant of GQDs would greatly influence the luminescence efficiency of GQDs. To obtain a sufficient ECL intensity, the ECL emission of the modified electrode was studied in test solution containing different amount of K2S2O8. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the ECL signals obviously increased with increasing K2S2O8 concentrations from 0.01 M to 0.03 M. When the concentration of K2S2O8 was higher than 30 mM, the ECL intensity changed slightly and reached relatively stable value. The reasonable explanation may be as follows. The excited state of GQDs* would react with excessive S2O8 2−, which would consume the GQDs*, thus inhibiting the increasing of ECL intensity. Similar quenching reactions were observed between carbon quantum dots (C*) and S2O8 2− [38]. Therefore, in order to achieve an effective amplification of ECL signals and avoid wasting chemicals, the 0.03 M of K2S2O8 was chosen as optimal coreactant concentration for further measurement.

The pH of the test solution was a significant factor affecting the performance of the biosensor. To investigate the influence of pH, the ECL responses of the biosensor were tested in pH range of 5.0–9.0. As recorded in Fig. 4B, the ECL intensity increased with the pH changing from 5.0 to 7.0, and then decreased with further increasing pH. The possible reason was that the proton was easily reduced to hydrogen at a lower pH, and it might inhibit the reduction of S2O8 2− [39], thus resulting in a low ECL intensity. While under a highly alkaline condition, the SO4 .- would be consumed by OH− [40,41], thus the ECL intensity decreased at a higher pH. The optimal ECL intensity was obtained at pH 7.0, therefore pH 7.0 of test solution was chosen for following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL response for Con A detection
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the ECL biosensor for Con A detection was investigated under the optimal condition selected. As shown in Fig. 4C, while the Con A concentrations increased from 0.5 pg/mL to 1.0 ng/mL, the ECL signals of the biosensor increased continuously. And there was a good linear correlation between the logarithmic value of the Con A concentrations and the ECL intensity. The linear regression equation was I = 1722.26 log c + 23217.24 (R = 0.9928). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to 0.16 pg/mL using the formulate LOD = kS b/m [38]. Here, the S b is the standard deviation of the blank signals, m is the analytical sensitivity, and k is the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3). To evaluate the performance of our prepared biosensor for Con A detection, the comparision of analytical performance between our work and others previous reports was performed. As listed in Table 1 , our work exhibited a lower detection limit. It was owing to the excellent ECL performance of GQDs, and the effective enhancement of CeO2@Ag NPs to the ECL intensity of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of prepared biosensor was tested by consecutive cyclic potential scanning. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the ECL signals were stable under continuously scanning for 8 circles with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.7%, which was due to the good chemical stability of GQDs and the stable interaction between Con A and GOx. And the long-time stability of prepared biosensor was also examined. The constructed biosensor was stored at 4 °C while not in use and tested once per 5 days. The results were recorded in Fig. S3, and it can be observed that the ECL responses of the biosensor retained 94% of its initial intensity after 30 days, indicating a good stability of the biosensor.

To investigate the reproducibility of the prepared biosensor, five biosensors prepared by the same method were tested under the same conditions. The RSD of ECL signals for those five biosensors was 4.5%, indicating an acceptable reproducibility.

In addition, to evaluate the selectivity of prepared sensor, the possible interferences such as BSA, phytohemagglutinin (PHA) and glucose (Glu) were investigated. In Fig. 5B, could see that the ECL responses of the biosensor for those interfering agents were weak, meanwhile a high ECL signal was observed in the presence of Con A. It was because that the special interaction between Con A and the carbohydrate endowed the biosensor a higher selectivity for Con A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensor to the detection of Con A in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Recovery experiments were used to assess the potential of practical application of the proposed sensor. The recoveries in human serum samples were achieved by standard addition method. The human serum samples were obtained from the Ninth People's Hospital (Chongqing, China) and then diluted 50 times using PBS (pH 7.0) for testing. The analysis results were recorded in Table 2 . The recoveries ranged from 95% to 104%, indicating an acceptable detection accuracy of prepared biosensor.

A solid-state ECL biosensor for sensitive detection of Con A was constructed using GOx-CeO2@Ag-GQD as ECL probe. Herein, the CeO2@Ag NPs as carrier not only could achieve the high loading of GQDs on the surface of CeO2@Ag NPs, but also could effectively enhance the ECL intensity of GQDs and improve the detection sensitivity. In addition, GQDs with low toxicity, excellent chemical inertness are suitable for biosensing. This strategy extended the application of GQDs in ECL sensing and also provided a potential approach for constructing a solid-state ECL biosensor based GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775122, 21775123, 51473136, 21575116), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing City (cstc2018jcyjAX0693), Medical Scientific Research Project of Health and Family Planning Commission of Chongqing (2017ZBXM018), Medical Scientific Research Major Project of Health and Family Planning Commission of Chongqing (2016ZDXM039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.10.084.
